skill.name: binary-re
skill.description: Guides binary reverse engineering workflows for executables, ELF files, firmware, and bytecode using triage, static analysis, dynamic analysis, and reporting phases.
skill.summary: Guided reverse engineering for binaries, executables, firmware, and bytecode.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell, filesystem, network, and blocker patterns, but review shows they are mostly documentation for legitimate reverse engineering workflows. The real risk is operational: the skill guides users through privileged tool setup and controlled execution of untrusted binaries, so it should publish only with a security warning.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted Binary Execution Workflow
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE with legitimate purpose. The skill instructs users to execute unknown binaries under QEMU, Docker, or debugging tools. It also requires human approval and sandbox checks, which reduces but does not remove risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The execution instructions are explicit, and the approval gates confirm the authors recognized the risk. The context is legitimate reverse engineering, not confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Setup Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE with legitimate purpose. The setup guide uses sudo package installation and Docker privileged mode for binfmt and QEMU emulation. These operations can alter host configuration and should require user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands clearly invoke sudo and Docker privileged mode. They are standard setup steps, but they expand host-level risk if copied without review.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer and Persistent Shell Configuration
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE with legitimate purpose. The tool setup section pipes a remote installer into bash and appends tool configuration to hidden home-directory files. This creates supply-chain and persistence risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky patterns are directly present and affect user shell or debugger startup files. The intent appears to be tool installation, so this is a warning rather than a block.

low_findings.title: Scanner Noise from Analysis Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for malicious intent. Many filesystem, proc, memory, and network hits are examples of what to inspect in a target binary, not code that exfiltrates data or modifies the host.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are markdown guidance and report examples. No evidence found that the skill itself contacts those addresses or reads those paths.

low_findings.title: Log Deletion Patterns Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The critical log-file findings point to sample report text describing a binary writing to a log file. No deletion command or cleanup instruction was found at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines use words such as log and writes, but they do not delete logs. This makes the static blocker match a clear false positive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Docker Privileged Mode
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends Docker privileged mode to register emulation handlers. This is a standard binfmt setup path, but it grants elevated container privileges.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact privileged Docker flag appears in multiple setup examples. The purpose is documented as emulator registration, not stealth or abuse.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Script Execution for Tool Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup guide includes a remote installer executed through bash. Users should inspect installer content and prefer pinned releases where possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The remote script execution pattern is explicit. The risk is supply-chain compromise, even though the tool being installed is common in debugging workflows.

dangerous_patterns.title: Host Configuration File Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup guide modifies hidden home-directory configuration files for radare2, GDB, and shell startup. This can persist behavior across future sessions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The affected files are startup or tool configuration files. The writes are documented setup steps, but persistence makes them security-relevant.

content.user_title: Analyze Binaries with Guided Reverse Engineering
content.value_statement: Binary analysis requires careful tool choice and safe execution controls. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured workflows for triage, static analysis, dynamic analysis, and reporting.
actual_capabilities: Guides triage for unknown binaries using file metadata, architecture checks, dependencies, and strings.
Provides static analysis workflows for radare2, Ghidra, functions, cross-references, and decompiled logic.
Describes dynamic analysis with QEMU, GDB, Docker, strace, and Frida after human approval.
Helps compare static and runtime evidence to validate behavior and reduce unsupported conclusions.
Produces structured reverse engineering reports with facts, hypotheses, confidence, and remaining unknowns.
limitations: It does not include the reverse engineering tools; users must install them separately.
Dynamic analysis of unknown binaries requires sandboxing, network controls, and explicit approval.
Windows PE workflows are limited because most examples focus on Linux ELF binaries.
Packed or heavily obfuscated binaries may need manual unpacking before useful analysis.

use_cases.title: Triage Suspicious Firmware
use_cases.description: Identify architecture, libraries, strings, and likely capabilities before deeper analysis of an unknown firmware binary.

use_cases.title: Debug Embedded Executables
use_cases.description: Use cross-architecture emulation and debugging guidance when source code is unavailable or production builds are stripped.

use_cases.title: Document Legacy Binary Behavior
use_cases.description: Turn scattered static and dynamic observations into a clear report for maintenance, migration, or risk review.

prompt_templates.title: Identify a Binary
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this binary at a triage level. Tell me the architecture, file type, dependencies, and first risks to inspect.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner triage of an unknown executable

prompt_templates.title: Map Static Behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform static analysis on this ELF file. Focus on functions, imports, strings, configuration paths, and likely capabilities.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Safe Runtime Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a safe dynamic analysis plan for this binary. Include sandbox setup, network controls, commands, and approval points.

prompt_templates.title: Synthesize Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a final reverse engineering report from our triage, static analysis, and dynamic analysis notes. Separate facts from hypotheses.

output_examples.output: The binary appears to target 32-bit ARM Linux with hard-float ABI. It is stripped, dynamically linked, and needs deeper review of network and configuration strings.

output_examples.input: Does this executable contact the network?
output_examples.output: Static evidence suggests client network behavior through socket and TLS-related imports. Runtime confirmation should happen in a network-blocked sandbox before any conclusion.
The report separates confirmed facts, supported hypotheses, unresolved questions, and recommended next steps. Each conclusion includes the evidence phase that supports it.

best_practices: Hash the artifact first and keep every result tied to that exact sample.
Start with static triage, then request approval before any runtime execution.
Record commands, observations, confidence, and unresolved questions during each phase.
anti_patterns: Do not run unknown binaries directly on a host workstation.
Do not treat strings or imports as confirmed behavior without supporting evidence.
Do not use privileged setup commands without reviewing their effect on the host.

faq.question: What file types does this skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on binaries, executables, ELF files, firmware blobs, Python bytecode, and marshal-style artifacts.

faq.question: Does it install radare2, QEMU, or Ghidra automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides setup guidance and commands, but users decide what to install and execute.

faq.question: Is dynamic analysis safe?
faq.answer: Dynamic analysis can be risky. The skill requires approval, sandbox checks, and network controls before execution.

faq.question: Can it analyze malware?
faq.answer: It can guide malware analysis, but users should use a dedicated lab environment with strong isolation.

faq.question: Does it support cross-architecture analysis?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes QEMU and Docker workflows for ARM, ARM64, MIPS, x86, and related Linux targets.

faq.question: What kind of final output can I expect?
faq.answer: It can produce a structured report with facts, hypotheses, confidence levels, evidence sources, and next steps.
